Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Defendant City And County Of San Francisco For Protective Order Regarding Plaintiff'S Disclosure Of Seventy-Five Non-Retained Experts; Request For Sanctions
Off Calendar Apr-05-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: RE: DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's For Protective Order Regarding Plaintiff'S Disclosure Of Seventy-Five Non-Retained Experts; Request For Sanctions.

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Mahsa Farid, Esq., of B & D LAW GROUP, APLC, (310) 424-5252, for Plaintiff.
MARGARET S. SCHROEDER, Esq., (415) 554-3843, Deputy City Attorneys, City and County of San Francisco, for Defendant.

Counsel for Plaintiff and counsel for Defendant stipulate to the matter being decided by the pro tem Judge. The pro tem Judge and counsels (all appearing remotely) authorize the clerk to sign the formal stipulation form on their behalf.

At the outset, counsel for the CCSF notes that no opposition to the court's tentative ruling was provided. The matter is argued. At conclusion of argument, the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: This case is set for trial on April 29, 2024. On March 11, 2024, Plaintiff served his disclosure of expert witnesses, identifying five retained experts and seventy-five (75) non-retained experts. Since then, Defendant has tried to have Plaintiff identify the non-retained experts whose expert opinions Plaintiff expects to offer in evidence at trial without success. Plaintiff's counsel stated that they reserved their right to call "any" of the seventy-five non-retained experts. Defendant requests that the Court enter a protective order requiring Plaintiff to produce a code-compliant expert disclosure that identifies the non-retained experts whom he intends to call at trial with a cap of ten expert witnesses. Defendant further requests the ability to depose those witnesses up until the jury has been sworn in this case. In the alternative, since Plaintiff has failed to provide a code-compliant list of non-retained experts, Defendant asks that the Court enter an evidentiary sanction precluding Plaintiff from calling non-retained experts to offer opinion testimony. Defendant further requests that the Court award $5,525 in sanctions for Plaintiff's counsel's abuse of the discovery process necessitating this motion and their failure to meet and confer in good faith. It appears that the deadline for providing expert declarations has come and gone. Since Plaintiff did not provide expert declarations with respect to the seventy-five non-retained experts, the non-retained experts cannot offer opinion testimony at trial. As far as monetary sanctions are concerned, Plaintiff has essentially reserved his right to call any of the non-retained expert witnesses as a percipient witness. No sanction is called for under such circumstances.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare a proposed order, approved as to form, and email it to the judge pro tem at schwartz@cmslaw.com.

Judge Pro Tem: Ken Korea. Clerk: R. Woods. Court Reporter: Raquel Sharp, T: 925.226.8222; E: raquel@transcriptlegal.com. (302/JPT)

Motion To File Under Seal
Off Calendar Mar-29-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. KATHERINE SMITH, Esq. and Atty. MAHSA FARID, Esq. of B&D Law Group, APLC (tel: 310-424-5252) for Plaintiff.
Atty. MARGARET SCHROEDER, Esq. and Atty. S. PATRICK KELLY, Esq. of Office of the City Attorney (tel: 415-554-3843) for Defendant.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's Motion To File Under Seal.

Matter not argued/contested. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's motion to seal is granted. The court concludes that: "(1) There exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the record; (2) The overriding interest supports sealing the record; (3) A substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; (4) The proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and (5) No less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest." (CRC 2.550(d).) Defendant seeks to seal plaintiff's medical records and testimony regarding his medical condition. Plaintiff's privacy right in his confidential medical information overcomes the right of public access. This overriding interest supports the sealing, plaintiff will be prejudiced without the sealing, defendant's motion is narrowly tailored, and there is no less restrictive means to achieve the overriding interest. The court will execute defendant's proposed form of order.
